did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically TernausNet-16 and Mask R-CNN, to perform segmentation of skin lesions and their attributes in dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2018 Challenge dataset. The study evaluated these deep learning models for dermatological diagnosis tasks.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work. It describes a comparative study evaluating two specific CNN models (TernausNet-16 and Mask R-CNN) on the ISIC 2018 Challenge dataset, detailing methodology, data preprocessing, training settings, and quantitative results (JSI and DSC metrics) to produce new findings on segmentation performance.

title: Segmentation of Skin Lesions and their Attributes in Dermatoscopic Images Based on Convolutional Neural Networks
journal: Revista de Informática Teórica e Aplicada - RITA
brief_summary: This study evaluated the effectiveness of two Convolutional Neural Networks, TernausNet-16 and Mask R-CNN, for segmenting skin lesions and five specific attributes in dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2018 Challenge dataset. The researchers addressed attribute imbalance by modifying the dataset and employed Jaccard Similarity Index (JSI) and Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) as evaluation metrics. The results demonstrated that Mask R-CNN significantly outperformed TernausNet-16, achieving a JSI of 82.57% and DSC of 84.76% for lesion segmentation. For attribute segmentation, Mask R-CNN achieved a JSI of 42.86% and DSC of 51.60%. Although Mask R-CNN required longer training times, the findings highlight its superior potential for improving melanoma segmentation accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary segmentation target in the study, delineated as a single connected region (ROI) in the ISIC 2018 Challenge dataset. The paper notes that while the dataset contains various types, the experiment focuses on the general segmentation of the lesion area rather than specific histological diagnoses, though it mentions improving melanoma segmentation effectiveness.
One of five specific dermoscopic attributes segmented by the models. Included in both original and modified datasets.

Mentioned in the context of the clinical importance of segmentation for diagnosing skin cancer and improving melanoma segmentation effectiveness, but not a specific class label used in the segmentation experiments.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion regions of interest (ROI) and five specific dermoscopic attributes (globules, milia-like cysts, negative networks, pigment networks, and streaks).
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates TernausNet-16 and Mask R-CNN for pixel-level instance segmentation. The models generate binary masks delineating the lesion boundaries and specific internal attributes. Performance is measured using Jaccard Similarity Index (JSI) and Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC), which assess the overlap between predicted masks and ground truth masks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 Challenge, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The study utilized two specific subsets from this challenge: one for skin lesion segmentation (ROI) and one for attribute segmentation. The dataset includes images in JPEG format (RGB) with ground truth masks in PNG format. The study also created a modified version of the attribute dataset to address class imbalance by downsampling the overrepresented 'Pigment Network' attribute and augmenting underrepresented attributes.

use.training: Used for training the TernausNet-16 and Mask R-CNN models. The original dataset was used for lesion segmentation training. For attribute segmentation, the modified dataset (with balanced attributes) was used for training.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process. The modified dataset was used for validation in attribute segmentation tasks.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the models. The test set was not modified and remained the same for both lesion and attribute segmentation evaluations.
use.other: The dataset served as the primary benchmark for comparing the performance of TernausNet-16 and Mask R-CNN against previous studies.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the overlap between predicted segmentation masks and ground truth masks by calculating the ratio of intersection to union.
To assess the similarity between predicted and ground truth masks by calculating twice the intersection divided by the sum of their areas.
To emphasize high-quality segmentations by considering only those with a JSI score of 65% or higher.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between TernausNet-16 and Mask R-CNN were based on quantitative metrics (JSI, DSC, TJI) and descriptive statements such as 'significantly outperformed' without statistical validation.

ISIC 2018 Challenge - Skin Lesion Segmen.notes: Mask R-CNN outperformed TernausNet-16 in all metrics. Mask R-CNN required 4 hours and 29 minutes for training and 12.87 GB VRAM, while TernausNet-16 required 2 hours and 54 minutes and 9.74 GB VRAM.

ISIC 2018 Challenge - Attribute Segmenta.notes: Mask R-CNN outperformed TernausNet-16. Mask R-CNN required 7 hours and 15 minutes for training and 15.34 GB VRAM, while TernausNet-16 required 4 hours and 38 minutes and 11.1 GB VRAM.
Mask R-CNN outperformed TernausNet-16. The modified dataset addressed class imbalance by downsampling 'Pigment Network' and augmenting other attributes.
Mask R-CNN outperformed TernausNet-16 across all individual attributes in the original dataset.

Mask R-CNN outperformed TernausNet-16 across all individual attributes in the modified dataset. Data augmentation improved performance for underrepresented attributes like 'Negative Network' and 'Streaks'.

architecture_used.pretraining: TernausNet-16 used pre-trained weights from the ImageNet dataset. Mask R-CNN used pre-trained weights from the COCO dataset.

TernausNet-16.description: An advanced segmentation model integrating components from VGG16 and U-Net.
TernausNet-16.encoder: VGG16 convolutional layers for feature extraction.
TernausNet-16.decoder: Deconvolutional layers to reconstruct segmented images while preserving spatial information.
TernausNet-16.connections: Skip connections between encoder and decoder.

Mask R-CNN.description: An object detection and segmentation model extending Faster R-CNN with a mask segmentation header.
components: Pixels classification network (assigns probability of belonging to object/background)
Mask regression network (estimates probability of pixel belonging to specific object)
Mask R-CNN.loss_function: Composite loss combining classification loss, bounding box regression loss, and mask segmentation loss.

TernausNet-16.learning_rate_reduction: Reduced by factor of 0.1 if validation loss did not improve for 5 consecutive epochs, with a minimum value of 0.00001.

classification_or_output_head.TernausNet-16: Deconvolutional layers generating segmented images (binary masks).
classification_or_output_head.Mask R-CNN: Pixels classification network and mask regression network.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 256 × 256 pixels using bilinear interpolation

Data augmentation for attribute segmentation (modified dataset): random cropping (75%), contrast adjustment (90%), brightness changes (50%), color changes (50%), affine transformations (50%), geometric transformations (50%), reflection (50%), and elastic transformations (50%)
Downsampling of the 'Pigment Network' attribute in the modified dataset to address class imbalance
Exclusion of images containing only the 'Pigment Network' attribute from the training and validation sets of the modified dataset

conclusions: Mask R-CNN significantly outperformed TernausNet-16 in segmenting skin lesions and their attributes in dermoscopic images.
Mask R-CNN achieved a Jaccard Similarity Index (JSI) of 82.57% and Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) of 84.76% for lesion segmentation.
When addressing attribute imbalance through dataset modification, Mask R-CNN achieved a JSI of 42.86% and DSC of 51.60% for attribute segmentation.
Data augmentation techniques improved generalization for underrepresented attributes, such as 'Negative Network' and 'Streaks', leading to substantial increases in JSI and DSC scores.

Downsampling the overrepresented 'Pigment Network' attribute helped mitigate model bias and create a more balanced dataset, although it resulted in a slight performance decrease for that specific attribute.
Mask R-CNN required significantly longer training times and higher GPU memory (VRAM) usage compared to TernausNet-16, highlighting a trade-off between performance and computational efficiency.
The results highlight the potential of Mask R-CNN for improving the effectiveness of melanoma segmentation.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 Challenge dataset, which limits the generalizability of findings to broader populations and other dermoscopic image sources.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance claims such as 'significantly outperformed' are based solely on descriptive metric comparisons (JSI, DSC).

Mask R-CNN demonstrated superior segmentation accuracy but required substantially higher computational resources, including longer training times (up to 7 hours 15 minutes) and higher GPU memory usage (up to 15.34 GB VRAM) compared to TernausNet-16.

The modified dataset used for attribute segmentation involved downsampling the overrepresented 'Pigment Network' class and applying data augmentation to underrepresented classes, which may introduce bias or limit direct comparison with models trained on the original imbalanced distribution.
The test set was not modified or augmented, preserving the original class imbalance, which may affect the evaluation of model robustness to rare attributes in real-world clinical settings.
The paper does not report external validation results, limiting the assessment of the models' clinical applicability and generalization to unseen data distributions.
